name: kv
context: Read, write, list, and delete key-value data in Cloudflare Workers KV — globally distributed eventual-consistency store
info: Workers KV is a globally distributed, eventually consistent key-value store optimized for high-read, low-write workloads. Data is replicated to all 300+ Cloudflare edge locations. Reads are fast (< 60ms globally) but writes take up to 60 seconds to propagate globally. Keys are strings up to 512 bytes, values are up to 25MB (text, JSON, ArrayBuffer, or ReadableStream). KV supports optional metadata (up to 1KB JSON) per key and TTL-based expiration. Access KV from Workers via bindings or from external systems via the REST API. Common uses: configuration storage, feature flags, caching, session data, and static asset serving.

sections.title: Creating and binding KV namespaces
sections.body: Create a namespace with `wrangler kv namespace create MY_KV`. Add the output binding to `wrangler.toml` under `[[kv_namespaces]]` with `binding`, `id`, and optionally `preview_id`. The binding name becomes the property name on the Worker's `env` object. Create a separate preview namespace for local development with `--preview`.

sections.title: Reading and writing values
sections.body: `env.MY_KV.put(key, value, options?)` writes a value. Options: `expirationTtl` (seconds), `expiration` (unix timestamp), `metadata` (JSON up to 1KB). `env.MY_KV.get(key, type?)` reads a value — type can be 'text' (default), 'json', 'arrayBuffer', or 'stream'. `env.MY_KV.getWithMetadata(key, type?)` returns both value and metadata. `env.MY_KV.delete(key)` removes a key.

sections.title: Listing and pagination
sections.body: `env.MY_KV.list({ prefix?, limit?, cursor? })` lists keys. Returns `keys` array with `name`, optional `expiration` and `metadata`. `list_complete` indicates if all results are returned. If false, use `cursor` for the next page. Maximum 1000 keys per list call. Always paginate with cursor for complete enumeration.

sections.title: Consistency model
sections.body: KV is eventually consistent with up to 60-second propagation delay after writes. Same-location reads are immediately consistent after writes. KV is optimized for read-heavy workloads (millions of reads, infrequent writes). Not suitable for counters, real-time state, or strong consistency requirements — use Durable Objects for those use cases.
